paper_title: The crossing number of the cone of a graph
summary: The conjecture that $f_s(k) = k + \sqrt{2}\,k^{3/4}(1+o(1))$ for simple graphs appears to remain open as of 2026. The original paper (published in SIAM J. Discrete Math. 32, 2018) established $f_s(k) = k + o(k)$. A 2021 follow-up by Ding and Huang computed additional specific small values of the related function $\varphi_s(k) = f_s(k) - k$ (proving $\varphi_s(6) = 5$ and $\varphi_s(7) = 6$), but did not resolve the asymptotic conjecture. No paper resolving the full asymptotic formula was found.

since_posted.title: A Note on the Crossing Number of the Cone of a Graph
since_posted.claim: Proves $\varphi_s(6)=5$ and $\varphi_s(7)=6$ (additional specific values of the cone crossing-number gap function), extending the table of known values from the original paper but leaving the asymptotic conjecture open.

crossing number cone graph "simple graph" k^{3/4} asymptotic conjecture
"Note on the Crossing Number of the Cone of a Graph" arXiv Graphs Combinatorics 2021
Ding Huang "cone of a graph" crossing number phi_s arXiv 2020 2021
notes: Full text of the Ding-Huang 2021 paper was behind a paywall; its basic facts (title, authors, journal, DOI, and the results on $\varphi_s(6)$ and $\varphi_s(7)$) were confirmed by multiple independent search-result snippets. The conjecture concerns the precise asymptotic $k + \sqrt{2}\,k^{3/4}(1+o(1))$ for simple graphs; the established bound $f_s(k) = k + o(k)$ is consistent with it but far weaker. No resolution of the full asymptotic statement was found in the indexed literature.
